top_profane.text:
Let me preface this by saying that I'm not particularly picky about this sort of thing. My fireplace is literally a hole in the wall with some bricks in the back and a chimney on top. We're not talking Ethan Allen massive glass-fronted gold-plated blah, blah—I just need something to burn.

You know, make fire.

Now if you're like me, you don't make fire to heat your house. It's purely cosmetic. It's nice to have something "warm and cozy" going over in the corner on a special occasion. So I bought a six-pack of these logs instead of the usual logs I buy because the price was right. Oops.

Here's the thing. These aren't made of sawdust bound with wax like the others. They are made of trash bound with wax. As in, boxes, newspapers, wrappers, whatever. Coated in wax and pushed together into a loggish sort of thing.

This doesn't immediately set off alarm bells. Sounds sensible, right? What could be more "green" than recycling? Reduce, reuse, recycle, right? But there are problems. Multiple problems. Serious problems.

1) They just plain don't start. You'll light the wrapper and it will burn away and then you'll have a pile of trash sitting in your fireplace. Like, it literally looks like someone's trash. Because it is. And you will then struggle for the next 20 minutes to get it to light. You'll burn tons of newspaper underneath, get one of those fire sticks with butane inside and hold it on against the edge of the thing for ages and whatever, and you'll get mayyyybe a tiny smolder... That goes out right away.

2) They're ugly as sin. It's trash. I mean, what could be better for ambiance than staring at smoldering trash in your living room? You can read the printing and identify brands and so on on all these shards of trash. It literally looks like you squished your kitchen trash into a sort of small, elongated pile, and tossed it in your fireplace. The guests will love it.

3) When you finally do get it started, the fire sucks. Even once it's "burning on all sides" we're talking a one-inch-high flame that is barely perceptible. It will go like that for hours and hours. Not a cheery, bright yellow, lapping flame, but what looks like a slow-burn warehouse trash fire. Not much heat, not much light. The trash itself is ugly, and then the fire is ugly on top of it. For hours. Hooray!

4) It's about as environmentally sound as hazardous waste. Okay, let's think about this. Most areas ban the burning of trash, even in wide open areas. Why? Because it smokes a lot, spews a lot of ash, and makes a terrible mess on the ground and in the air. And these are no different. They smoke like a trash fire in your yard, because—let's be honest—they're a trash fire in your fireplace. The amount of smoke is incredible. And as fuel burns away, bits of the paper and cardboard will waft up, black and ashen, into the room or around in the fireplace and finally up the chimney. Just like at any trash fire. Look ma, it's just like back home on the farm when we used to burn off the crud piles!

In short, these suck. They make your living room look like a narcotic squat house with a trash fire for warmth going in the corner. The flame itself is anemic and barely enough to provide any heat or light. They take tons and tons and tons of investment on your part to get started burning. And they pollute like nobody's business.

If you're looking for an ambiance fire, you've come to the wrong place.

If you're interested in experiencing the joys of working class rural America when there's a whole pile of trash to get rid of and you can't afford to fix the truck to drive it to the dump, so you stand around with your friends and a beer and watch a bunch of crap slowly burn and crud up the yard with ash and smoke—well, click away. These are just what you're looking for.

*UPDATE*

Aaaaand this just happened: See, it's Thanksgiving Day here and one of the things that we tend to do is keep a fire going in the fireplace for the day.

Now normally, my seven-year-old daughter looooves having a fire going in the fireplace. But she just came to me about two minutes ago and said, "Daddy, today's fire is ugly and sort of scary. Can I just pour some water in there and put it out?"

Sort of sums it up.

Not sure I'll burn the other four of these. More likely, they'll go out—with no small hint of irony—as trash. I know, I know, you're not supposed to put flammable stuff in the trash. But it's not like these burn anyway!

Worst fire logs ever.
top_profane.title: Great way to turn your fireplace into a back yard in the boonies.

top_profane.text: I was extremely skeptical of this … I mean let’s face it, it’s just a giant container of the stickiest substance on earth lmfao… but when I tell you this stuff works… IT WORKS..!!! Here’s the story!! …… So it all started when A pair of pigeons laid two eggs on my balcony I could very well of just threw them off my balcony and got rid of the problem from the beginning but I was trying to be a decent human being and decided to let them stay and raise the little chicks… Big mistake I should’ve been like the rest of society and been a shitty human being a.k.a. throwing the eggs off the balcony… had I known what was to come… Approximately 21 days later the eggs hatched give or take a few days I did a bunch of research online it said from start to finish it should take no longer than three months total time from the time of conception for the eggs to hatch grow up and fly away and leave the nest… this is the prime reason I do not believe everything I read on the Internet… Five months later I had a family of pigeons living on my balcony they had taken over shit everywhere all over everything and refused to leave no matter what I did they were not afraid of me due to the fact that I gave them water and birdseed when they were first born keep in mind this was 3 to 4 months ago those little bastards not forget those 2 to 3 days of birdseed and water and they decided to hold onto Hope that I would bring it out to them again and they have held onto that hope for the last three months going on 4… I decided I needed to do something immediately but I did not want it to be something heinous and I did not want to hurt them even though I secretly didn’t want to strangle them at times they were loud dirty disgusting and disrespectful… not to mention I was on the second story The two chicks that the parents had refused to learn how to fly so they just walked around my porch shitting and cooing all day waiting for their parents to bring them food… Refusing to flying off and leave the nest, just wanting to get taken care of, get fed , and be in a free shelter that they did not have to build and eat food they did not have to work for!!! kind of like the millennials of today… I was not above throwing these little bastards off the balcony and giving them a flying lesson myself but as I said I am in an apartment building and I don’t think the neighbors would appreciate that it might be frowned upon… Plus I have a two year-old daughter who loves to look out the sliding glass door at the birds… I don’t think me throwing her pets off the balcony is exactly the type of childhood memory I want her to have lmfao .. eventually I ended up putting a towel over each one and carrying it downstairs and placing it on the ground… I thought this would get rid of my pigeon problem boy was I wrong lol the very next day I go to the balcony to clean it up from the months of nasty pigeon crap that had been festering all over it I wanted my porch back I wanted my sanity back… And lo and behold those two freaking flightless pigeons learned how to fly overnight apparently and were sitting on the porch once again… so I took them downstairs again… And again… And again… And again… This went on for the next week straight multiple times a day… so let’s fast forward about two weeks after I begin trying different methods to get rid of these birds as I said nothing worked the pigeon spray where it stinks to high heaven and is supposed to make them not want to be around it they did not care they walked directly on top of it hell they slept on it… The pigeon spikes they did not care they flew over them and would perch on anything else that the spikes were not on… just when I was looking up BB guns on Amazon I came across this pigeon glue and I thought what the hell it’s cheap I’ll try one last hurrah before I get rid of these damn birds forever and ever! I’m glad I read the reviews first or I would’ve for lack of a better phrase fu$&ed myself!! If you buy this product and I cannot stress this enough please please please please be sure to put down tape preferably weather resistant tape on the surface of anything that you were going to put this glue on because I don’t see how he would get it off of anything once it’s on there that is how messy this stuff is I read another persons review who said they put down painters tape and put this pigeon glue on it brilliant idea only thing was I could not have bright blue painters tape hanging off of my balcony so I got clear weather resistant scotch tape The thickest one I could find to take up the most space and use the least amount of tape I covered the entire top part of my balcony with it and every single surface I could find on my balcony which wasn’t much considering I had nothing out there on the porch anymore since I cleared it all out hoping that would get rid of the birds (another failed attempt by the way).. so once I was finished covering the balcony railings with the tape, a porch light cover with the tape, the little trashcan I had outside for my babies stinky diapers with the tape etc.. I then proceeded to cover every surface I could with this glue of the Gods!!  (BTW: live in Las Vegas it gets about 110 to 112° or hotter on a regular basis in the summer here so needless to say this stuff does not melt lol it also gets pretty cold here in the winter this stuff does not freeze either at least not that I’ve seen it’s extremely weather resistant just thought I’d throw that in there) so once I told the little squatters  one last Fu#k you and the finger before heading inside I thought to myself if this doesn’t work I’m moving… The very next day I was taking my daughter to her doctor appointment I looked up on my balcony and I saw the pigeons swarm my balcony as if to mock me.. I was pissed I thought the product had failed… I even saw one of them on top of the light which I also covered in the sticky stuff if you recall what I said earlier lol as I watch them I also noticed they were trying to twist their feet but it kept getting stuck… I noticed the reason they were flocking and swarming was because they were trying to help the ones that were stuck in the glue LMAO and we’re getting stuck themselves by the time I got back from the doctors there was not a pigeon left on my balcony they were all on the neighbors balcony LMAO and they were all touching their feet and walking funny I’m guessing because there feet were sticking as they were walking!! Lmfao ever since then I saw two maybe three more times a pigeon land on the balcony railing and immediately fly off this was on the second day but ever since that second day I have not seen one pigeon come near my balcony!!!! I see them on my neighbor‘s balcony all the time as a matter fact I believe they laid eggs over there and started another family LOL but as far as my balcony goes not a pigeon in sight lol!!! I recommend this product 100% I have told all my friends about it as pigeons are a big problem in Las Vegas!! So if you’re ever looking at this and wondering does it really work I vouch for it and I would buy it again and again and again!!

top_profane.text:
Pros:
- Correct size and Thread to replace the defective breakers on the hose cocks.
- Material Quality was the same as the OEM parts.
- Easy to install if you do not destroy the threads on the hose cock when removing the vacuum breaker.
- Comes with rubber gasket so no other parts needed to install.
- Very fast delivery.

Cons:
- While less expensive than the OEM parts, still somewhat pricey for a basic turned metal piece. The price was of this part was the lowest I could find so it is really not a negative.

I purchased a building that had the hose cocks with external vacuum breakers which were broken and leaking. Replacement vacuum breakers are expensive, prone to failure, and have a tendency to leak. I took the defective vacuum breakers off and replaced them with these pass through adapters. The water flow rate is much improved and there are no leaks to waste water.

Be sure to choose the correct size adapter to fit the thread size and pattern on your hose cock. Also, some vacuum breakers are pinned or have screws attaching the vacuum breaker to the hose cock which need to be removed before the vacuum breaker can be removed. If you just try to unscrew those from the hose cock you can destroy the threads or destroy the hose cock entirely.
top_profane.title: JL Brand SM01 Vacuum Breaker Adapter - Same Product Just Less Expensive

top_profane.text:
My spouse and I waited almost 4 years after we purchased our home before we were able to purchase our Aerobin. I had wanted a compost bin for several decades as I knew that we could put our kitchen scraps and yard clippings in one and it would allow us to reduce the amount of trash that needed to be picked up. I also knew we could then make our own plant food and soil for our yard thereby not having to transport bags of soil from another location to our house. I also knew I would know what we were making our soil out of, in our case organic egg shells, cardboard from 100% post consumable recyclable toilet paper rolls, and similar soybean ink printed envelopes mixed with our yard trimmings that we never spray and our other vegetarian kitchen scraps with foods like organic soy porridge (from the making of homemade soy milk,) coffee grounds, tea bags, and lots of homemade bread crumbs. I knew I did not want to smell my compost bin so after my spouse and I got our home I heard about the Aerobin 400 and how it’s unique structure meant I could place it right next to my house and not smell it. Finally almost four years later, and several decades looking forward to being able to own my own home and land complete with a compost bin it is true. The amount of waste we made before getting our Aerobin was already so minimal I wondered how we could improve in a positive fashion more than what we already had? Most of our food is homemade and it is all vegetarian. We use mason jars to store leftovers. We do not eat out. We do not buy soda, we make it. We make our own Frappacinos and lattes and low fat ice cream sodas. We even brew our own beer and make our own yogurt and soy milk. We do not use paper napkins, plastic straws (we use stainless steel,) paper towels nor tissues. Outside of our yard clippings we had very little trash. It took less than a week after we set up our Aerobin that it did make even more of a difference.

My spouse calculated how many bricks we would have to purchase to raise our Aerobin up 3 bricks high as it must be raised up above ground level. He found out it took 96 bricks to do this. I am sharing this information in case you purchase an Aerobin so you know how many bricks to buy at the store to raise it above the ground. It could be raised up as little as two bricks but since I am 5’9 inches tall and my spouse, he is over 6 feet we raised our Aerobin up three bricks. Also, if you buy an Aerobin know that you will need to purchase hay to place on the bottom along with compost starter or moist soil. Instead of compost starter we used 40 pounds of bagged soil with manure. Hay can be purchased at local feed stores or online and shipped from American Pet. I recommend purchasing ½ to ¼ bale along with the compost starter when buying the Aerobins, in addition to buying bricks or longer stones. We are never selling our Aerobin, nor are we giving it away.

I will share some things I did not know about the Aerobin 400 before I purchased mine. All Aerobin 400s are the same style and size so if you are going to purchase one of your own you should read this. I found out it seemed best to buy the additional hose to attach to the outlet at the bottom that pours the liquid plant food. Make sure there is easy access to both doors of the Aerobin. As I quickly found out a few insects did get inside, and I did not place them there. There are small holes at the bottom and they must have gotten in that way. By opening the doors of the Aerobin regularly, the insects can jump out and escape safely. I would not recommend getting one if the person or person(s) were not able to open the doors regularly to let any trapped insect out. See as compost is added it might make it more difficult for them to get out the way they came in, (through the bottom holes,) and it gets hot in there and it is dark. So, for ethical reasons they must be able to get out. Also, note that small flies are attracted to the top of the Aerobin. Predators such as spiders must not be allowed to ‘hang out,’ around the Aerobin. I had to remove a spider that was lurking on mine.

There was a little bit of a smell after a few days. I found I just needed to layer in some yard clippings and the smell disappeared.

When owning a composter one must be careful when placing yard clippings in that they do not contain insects as it would not be ethical to trap an insect in a composter. Since I already was doing that before throwing out my yard clippings before in the trash bag,( I did not want to trap any in a landfill or trash truck,) this has not been a burden to me. I had even stopped using sealed vacuum bags as I was concerned about this ethical issue.

Also with the Aerobins they recommend backflushing the outlet with water but I do not since I would worry about flooding insects. Instead I will make sure I pour the liquid plat food out weekly and make sure it does not dry out by adding things like the mayo water and soy porridge so I will not have to backflush it.

At first I was thinking about purchasing a compost pail but I try to percolate and see if I really have to buy whatever as I am mindful about resources and also want to be ethical and never downgrade my quality of life or others. For example, I chose not to have children for many reasons including my spouse and I had been burdened by others who had children a great deal. The other thing is obviously there is not a shortage of humans, as I knew many that were in apartments or condos or rentals. It seems a wise, ethical, caring and loving decision not to have children.

After first getting our Aerobin started my spouse and I had some liquid plant food to put on our lawn/garden within one week. Soil is also already being made. The pamphlet says it can take up to three months for this to happen. I think ours was faster because of the hay, soil with cow manure, and the hot Turkish coffee grounds I throw in. Instead of buying a compost pail I made one. This is how I did it:
I took a small light weight plastic bucket I own, made a few very small vent holes in it with one of my veggie kabob skewers, then covered the holes with a piece of cheesecloth I then affixed with museum wax. My spouse and I place our compostables such as coffee grounds, egg shells, etc., in that plastic bucket I labeled with liquid chalk and a sticker from a set I bought that works also to label mason jars. That is the bucket I take and pour in our Aerobin that my spouse and I own daily.

All sorts of food I wasted before was no longer wasted since getting my Aerobin. For example, I discovered I could take waste water from our bread pans and pour it in empty mayonnaise jars, shake them and pour the liquid mayo waste in my compost pail adding more nutrients to the soil it makes. Crumbs on the counter that used to go in the hand vac and the trash my spouse and I sweep into our easily lifted to the counter home made lightweight compost pail to be placed later in our Aerobin.

2/11/18 Update:
Almost 4 months of using my Aerobin has caused me to discover several things about using and owning these type of composters. After I place my kitchen scraps in I cover them with leaves or clippings from my lawn. This way there is no odor, even when I open the lid. Note I am a vegetarian so my kitchen scraps reflect that and maybe that is why there is no ‘trashy’ odor when the lid is opened. Every so often, I open both of the doors as this is recommended. It has not produced much compost yet, but I can see it is working on it as the level of contents drops pretty quickly and I have not filled it completely so I can continually use it each day. I discovered the tube my spouse installed so we could get the liquid plant food easier became blocked. The manufacturer recommended back flushing the tube with a garden hose but as I thought deeply about it I disagreed. I have insect activity in the composter, therefore I felt it would be unethical and flood the insects if I did this. So I searched for another solution to keeping these tubes clear. A pipe cleaner would work, but before I purchased one I considered carefully, what I already had. Could I do it using something I have in a new and different way? I had already taken one of my garden hoops and converted it into a wreath frame for a homemade wreath. I could use another of my garden hoops to keep the tube cleared, the ends of the hoops are dull. That is exactly what I did and am doing. You might consider reusing the things you have and see if that can be done rather than buying something else. For example, instead of buying more coasters I use saucers. I also am telling you if you buy an Aerobin and have insect activity in it please be ethical and do not back flush the liquid plant food port flooding the composter. The amount of plastic trash bags my spouse and I use has been reduced so significantly that we now are able to have even more variety in our vegetarian diet since we no longer need to buy as many bags. A tip, if you buy in bulk at stores like Costco and get a case of toilet paper know the plastic it comes wrapped in can be reused as a trash bag liner. I do this with my Seventh Generation Toilet paper made from all recycled materials.
2/25/18 AMAZING DISCOVERY UPDATE by Julie Sands
Guess what? I opened one of the side doors on my Aerobin to find very healthy looking MUSHROOMS are growing inside it as it makes soil. This tells me that Aerobins can also be used as giant mushroom growers if seeded with mushroom spores and given only vegetarian composting material. So upon reflecting on this and the fact that in the space of one week (I open the side doors once a week,) mushrooms appeared I know that now is a great time for me to get mushrooms started in my Aerobin for harvesting and eating. The mushrooms that are already there appear to be white button mushrooms but I will not eat them. I purchased a Baby Bella (small brown pizza mushrooms,) growing kit. . I placed it in my Aerobin at the coolest level but was unable to grow that type of mushrooms in it. I do conceive of another invention just for growing mushrooms year round in back yards about the size of the Aerobin.
UPDATE 5/12/18
Seven months after I began using my Aerobin I finally got some fantastic composted soil from my Aerobin. I took it out from one of the side doors. It was a small yield. There is more in the other side door but I have not removed it yet. I used it to start some seeds in some plastic egg trays I am re-using as seed starters. The remainder I placed in one of my planters. It still was not enough to top it off. Perhaps now that I removed the composted soil the remainder will compost quicker since there is more air-flow at the bottom. I noted there were some small insects in the soil and so I do not recommend using Aerobin composted soil for indoor plants. I did not harm the insects but left them in the seed starters and will transfer them to the 4-inch pots when I transfer my seedlings to them.
UPDATE 7/31/18
Just got some of my second batch of compost yesterday. There was more in the Aerobin this time. I am writing to ask those that compost to be careful with the tools they use to retrieve the compost. For example I could not use my garden rake or spade, etc., since there are insects and worms in my Aerobin and that might injure them. I had to come up with a work around. In my case I found a very bendable flexing plastic cooking spoon coupled with a small bendable used yogurt container to help scoop the soil onto the spoon worked best. I hold a lightweight bendy plastic bucket under my composter to easily catch all the soil I retrieve from it. Since I have it raised up on bricks I set my low garden chair beside it when I do this to make it even easier. I use kitchen style plastic gloves that come up above the wrist I rewash to do this. There is no odor from it.
UPDATE 8/2/18
Great day! I checked the other side of my Aerobin for compost and I found out there is lots and lots of high quality soil in it. So much so that I realize my Aerobin will now meet most of my soil needs. No more toting 30-pound bags of soil from the store! I can just scoop out what I need from my Aerobin. I still will purchase the lighter weight Wonder Soil once in a while for some of my planters that require a soil-less growth medium. But my and my spouse's Aerobin will take care of my spouse’s and my regular soil needs (for planting, topping off planters, etc.,) from now on. It has been a little over nine months since we began composting.-Julie Sands
GENERAL INFO ON COMPOSTING UPDATE 8/24/18
I have some tips I have discovered since owning my Aerobin and also owning my home and land and tending to it. For I spent hours ‘winterizing’ my sensitize plants. I was unhappy with the garden cloth I was using and began to look for a different solution. During that time I and my spouse used to spend hours raking leaves, placing them in MANY plastic trash bags and they (those bags with leaves we put out to be picked up,) were picked up by a waste removal (sanitation) truck. Then I was even spending time placing wood chip mulch at the base of some plants that had to have a leaf mulch base that happens when my deciduous trees shed their leaves. As I was picking up leaves I began noticing how the wind seemed to be artfully placing them across my yard so I could see how incredibly helpful they are. They are at the base of delicate plants requiring a ‘mulch’ blanket for example. The plants are quite healthy through winter with just the mulch of leaves. So I changed my and my spouse’s behavior. Instead of picking up leaves during autumn, I selectively picked some (only one bagful,) up but not others. I then tried something as I thought about those trash pickups that were picking up plastic bags of leaves and not being able to dump them out of the bags. I left that trash bag of leaves closed but did not put it out to be picked up. I opened up the bag in the spring and lo and behold, I had around a 20 pound plastic trash bag filled with GORGEOUS perfectly composted topsoil! I placed it in some of my planters. Shed leaves are much better utilized if kept at home. My system involves just letting deciduous leaves fall and not bagging or raking them. Some I spread around lightly just with my feet, it is quick and easy. Some I collect and place in my planters as mulch for winter. The leaves disintegrate and turn into soil/plant/tree nourishment just as it starts to warm up when they know their ‘blanket’ is no longer needed. Any that have fallen on some concrete areas of my yard also disintegrate and turn into soil and I just take my spade and scoop the soil up into my planters. The plants I used to cover with the winter cloth do much better with my leaf system also. Know if you are keen to start composting but can’t buy an Aerobin without putting it on a pay over time credit card (debt) do not despair and do not go into debt. Well, if you do not have any back or knee injuries an easy home made composter can be fashioned from those big plastic trash cans with lids and wheels. These are less expensive to purchase than an Aerobin and can also be purchased on Amazon new. You can throw kitchen scraps in trash cans with lids and wheels, but I do not recommend placing dairy in there or even meat to keep odor away. Then, in a matter of some months that trash will have turned into soil with NO turning of the compost required, just like a sealed bag of leaves. Why do I suggest the kind with wheels? Because soil is very heavy and laying the trash can down to get the soil out will be easy if the trash can has wheels. Otherwise, if you have one without wheels place it where you can use leverage to tip it over once it is filled with soil, just remember to tape the lid shut first. Then just remove the tape and scoop out the top soil. It’s really that simple. People that already own large plastic bins with lids can also use them as a composter if they want to make their own soil. Those that are handy at recycling wood pallets (experienced or self do it carpenter types,) could if they want to also make a composter out of these by just thinking about it a bit and can even build them to be easy to use even with a back or knee problem. If you have a back issue like I do I recommend just waiting and saving up until you can buy yourself a new Aerobin. I waited several years before I was able to buy mine.
UPDATE 1/29/19
I love my Aerobin. I have potting soil whenever I want always ready. I found out many other people that also own Aerobins end up buying additional units if they have the room for them. I keep mine at least 3/4s full and it is often almost maxed out though I can always fit my daily kitchen scraps in it. If I have more yard clippings that don’t fit I burn them and use them as fuel for my grill type of thing. Since mine has insects in it I found if I go to get soil it is best to give a knock on the side door I will open, then wait a few seconds so that the insects can move to the other side of the Aerobin. Before I did this some insects would be on the inside of the doors when I opened them and it was gross. I do not use traditional metal garden tools to gather potting soil or to move the compost down since that would possibly be hazardous to the insects. Instead, I use things like flexible plastic parmesan cheese containers, non-fat yogurt containers which I use like a plastic cup to dig the potting soil loose from the sides, etc. rather than using a sharp or too hard metal garden fork. This way, insects are not injured. I use a plastic flexible spoon handle to poke down the stuck leaves at the top and to loosen the potting soil as that bends and flexes to prevent injuries. I never flood the composter and it works great. I also recommend those that own those composters with handles to stop turning them as the heavy weight of the soil might not be the best thing if there are insects in there. The compost will still break down, it just might take a little longer, but will then be on a regular schedule so they will have enough soil from those also but they also no longer have to do that daily chore of turning them.
-Julie Sands
top_profane.title: Things I have thought about since my spouse and I own an Aerobin of our very own...

top_profane.text:
I love this thing! It really is so much fun. My son has a different 2.0 model and I just couldn’t stand it anymore and had to have my own. It is just too much fun shooting flies and other tiny pests. During the summer months we can be inundated with flies here. All I have to do is just sit down on the deck and they seek me out. Possibly due to the dogs and chickens leaving plenty of calling cards plus all the livestock around. Unfortunately, I didn’t order my gun until it got cold and my choice of victims was sparse. I was reduced to skulking around the backyard in search of involuntary targets, but it was more like fishing than catching. Even so, when I did come upon some tenacious multi legged creature smugly taunting me I gleefully took him out. My kill ratio was about 80% including one double tap. One kill shot was four feet away. I think I could have done better, but it was windy.

It’s so simple to operate. Fill the hopper with regular table salt. Cock it. Flip off the safety and pull the trigger. It shoots a pinch of salt destroying your enemy. When you cock it the sight pops up letting you know it’s ready to go. Each time you cock it the safety resets. This is kind of a pain, but you get used to it. If you try squeezing the trigger – not pulling it back quick enough – the safety will actually reset. So pull that trigger. Don’t slowly squeeze. Note that you don't have to wait until you cock it to disable the safety. You can click the safety off, cock it and the safety will remain off - unless you do that slow squeeze the trigger thing.

To improve my accuracy I purchased a laser sight. There are a number of designs available, but the one I picked has the best design. It has nice knobs to adjust windage and elevation, uses a lithium battery and has a very nicely designed pressure switch. The pressure switch is perfect for not wasting your lasers battery. Just press it with your thumb and the laser is on allowing you to zero in on your tormentor. Release pressure on the switch and the laser is off. To get the one that I got go here on Amazon:

https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B07DQLZHMD/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o00_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1

It cost a little more than most of the others, but I feel it’s worth it.

But, I’m supposed to be reviewing the bug-a-salt so….

Note that I only gave four stars. The gun is worth five except there is a new 3.0 model available now that is designed so much better. I only discovered the 3.0 about a week after receiving my 2.0. I went ahead and ordered the 3.0 too. If I didn’t have that then this 2.0 would have gotten the five stars. The differences?

The 3.0 is gun metal gray (with the standard strategic and lawfully required orange).
The 2.0 comes in several colors including camo depending on the design you choose, but not this gray.

The 3.0 is smaller and lighter than the 2.0.

The 3.0 has more power than the 2.0.

The 3.0 has a tighter shot pattern than the 2.0.

The 3.0 has a bigger and better designed salt hopper than the 2.0.

The 3.0 has a much better designed safety. You press it once and it stays on where as on the 2.0 you must click the safety again every time you cock the gun.

The 3.0 has a better sight design than the 2.0.

The trigger on the 3.0 is lighter and easier to pull than the 2.0.

Where the 2.0 is good at knocking the life out of flies and turning your misery into joy, the 3.0 is capable of splattering there souls to oblivion turning your joy into glee.

Don’t get me wrong. The 2.0 is a fantastic gun. It’s pretty deadly when it comes to flies. It's certainly more accurate than a regular fly swatter that those big eyed buggers can see coming and has a higher success rate than those electric fly swatters too. (Although those electric boys are kind of satisfying when they connect. BzzzZAP! Sizzle sizzle.) No doubt that you would be perfectly happy with this guy and have the time of your life making the world safe from the evil little denizens of the air. I just favor the 3.0 over the 2.0. If you get this guy there is no way you will be disappointed unless you’re one of those people that has to have the latest and the greatest. If that’s not the case then don’t think twice. The only time you might get frustrated is when the little devils go into stealth mode. You know, they land on your hand. You reach for your gun and they take off and disappear. You wait. You finally put your gun down and they land on your forehead. Sometimes it's like they disappear into the forth dimension and only reappear when you put down your weapon of choice. Despite the flies sneaky ways, you'll have the last laugh. This is the best entertainment you’ll ever have at your backyard bar-b-cue. You can even use it to salt your burger.
top_profane.title: You can even use it to salt your burger.

top_profane.text: So I was loving this wheelbarrow as it is a multi-functional wheelbarrow that can actually move heavy items through doorways in the house. It doesn't steer worth a damn, and the seat doesn't attach, it's just a hard plastic seat that slides on groove only in the middle, otherwise it will tip off if your weight isn't centered perfectly.  But that is the the wagon kit, not the turd of wheelbarrow this crap ended up being.  So I put it all together, directions sucked, but it was pretty common sense.  Once it was together, I was in love... I grabbed 4 fifty pound bags of solar salt, and carted them through the grass perfect, jumped back onto the sidewalk and the flat surface conversion wasn't an issue.  I then pulled it backwards up the step into the patio doors and that was a struggle.  The little wheels going up a 90 degree 6 inch step with a 200 lb load had to be yanked hard diagonally one wheel at a time, but no big deal, it's like my cheap little portable 2 wheel dolly like that.  The absolute disgust came when I decided to leave the salt bags in the wheelbarrow overnight so I could come back in the morning to clean out the brine tank.  Brine tank cleaned, and I went to move the salt to the softener, but after it rolled less than a foot....thump...thump!  The damn cart won't push even anywhere?  I set it down, spin the never flat tires around and both damn tires now have flat spots from the weight compressing them.  Who came up with that idea you god damn engineering morons.  I bought the most expensive small wheelbarrow on the market, and the tiny crap tires can't even let this piece of crap work!!!

top_profane.text:
I am incredibly dissatisfied with this purchase for two reasons:
1) I placed too much emphasis on the overall customer RATINGS and boy can I just tell you that AMAZON customers are truly all over the map on the subjective "softness" "luxuriousness" and just the general level of comfort supposedly offered by this poorly designed & even more poorly constructed bath pillow... PLEASE TAKE RAVE REVIEWS WITH A GRAIN OF SALT. Literally 1 tiny grain. Then toss it over your shoulder and look at OTHER OPTIONS. (Seriously)

2) The name GORILLA might seem to some as befitting of a heavy duty, high quality and exceedingly durable product. Well, this GORILLA GRIP Original Spa Bath Pillow is only befitting of the GORILLA GRIP name in that it feels like it was made FOR AN ACTUAL GORILLA to recline upon....not a human with tender skin, in need of some PADDING between the nape of the neck and the enameled cast iron bathtub, nor just the very most basic pillowy-ness that this awful product promises to provide.

Specifically....this dumb thing is made in China and it is truly the lowest quality materials one would NEVER hope to find upon opening the crummy outter plastic wrapper. It is QUITE LITERALLY just two thin layers of CHEAP WHITE VINYL PLASTIC that are HEAT WELDED & cut together (fused under pressure & die cut in one stamping) with a single 1" layer of low density polyurethane foam sandwiched in between. The back of this multi-segmented "pillow" is enhanced with additional rings of the same crappy vinyl that are melted and pressed into the pillow to create each of the suction cup "holders," which are just stiff plastic dimples roughly 3/8" deep and 1/2" around (you can insert the tip of your index finger) and lemme tell ya...... They SUCK AT GRIPPING! Period. They don't grip the suction cups firmly enough to PREVENT the suction cups from pulling right off the back of the pillow....but sadly half the time the suction cups DO STAY ATTACHED to the pillow, they just WON'T STAY ADHERED TO THE DAMN TUB. The remaining half of the time, they pull out of the pillow and STAY FIRMLY ATTACHED TO THE TUB. So....gorilla grip is truly a misnomer and ends up being more maddening than anything else.

But WAIT! There's more...
You get not only a crappy vinyl plastic slightly puffy bathmat for a "pillow" but you ALSO GET THE MOST OFFENSIVE CHEMICAL / PLASTIC / RUBBERY COMBINATION SMELL that will most assuredly NOT go away! It hasn't gone away in nearly 4 months.....and the smell is so bad that even when stored INSIDE THE CABINET UNDERNEATH THE BATHROOM SINK, I can STILL smell the totally unmistakable odor of extremely cheap summertime flotation devices (or an air mattress) that you'd find poolside! (You know how well those pieces of junk hold up...so imagine this *upgraded* with 4 pieces of the most meager foam padding. Yeah. It sucks and stinks....but it will also scratch the hell outta your delicate bits if the damn pillow shifts abruptly and the sharp edges of the cut vinyl happen to find your skin instead of the bathtub or tile.

And yes.....it is WATERPROOF. ISH. At least at first. So.....let's really be super sensible here for one FINAL and HOPEFULLY CONVINCING POINT:

Have you ever tried dunking a basketball or other completely SEALED but INFLATED object and holding it under water? Ever tried FORCING IT TO STAY UNDER THE SURFACE OF THE WATER?? How well does THAT work out before you give up the INSANE and UNTENABLE attempt at sinking the unsinkable??

Well, after you draw a nice warm bath and the level of the water is high enough that it FIGHTS WITH THE AIR THAT IS TRAPPED INSIDE THE FOAM PADDING WITHIN EACH OF THE 4 SECTIONS OF THIS DAMN PILLOW......I'll give you one guess as to WHICH wins! The pillow or the water. My bet is ON THE WATER. Every stinking, aggravating, frustrating, God forsaken time.....the GORILLA GRIP crap pillow POPS RIGHT UP OUTTA THE WATER and EVEN UPON LEANING INTO IT WITH EVERY LAST POUND ON MY BONES, the stupid piece of junk just slips, slides, twists and otherwise REFUSES TO COOPERATE in any way shape or form!

Useless.

Even the cat and all 3 dogs will have NOTHING to do with it. It literally stinks and it is not the least bit PILLOW-LIKE.

I'll add photos later. Not that they'll do anything to improve this review. They will however prove that this thing does not HOLD UP under even rare usage by only one person in the household.

Thanks for reading and I honestly hope like hell that my thoroughly UNABASHED and EXTREMELY CANDID review provides you with the knowledge and courage to LEAVE THIS DAMN GORILLA GRIP P.O.S. TO THE ACTUAL GORILLAS. (Sorry, gorIllas....nothing personal. Honest)
top_profane.title: GORILLA GRIP.....IT'S ANYTHING BUT THAT and WORSE than mere words express!!!

top_profane.text:
I am SO done with Hayward. I bought my 2nd Hayward filter 1 year ago via Amazon, and while I had some minor concerns over my first one, overall it kept my pool clean. I bought a second because we moved and installed a pool at the new house. Now the bad stuff....
The lint trap on the first one was so tight to close, that I risked cracking the lid every time I tried to open it. Problem solved on the new filter. But ..
The shut off valve that comes with the filter is the same crappy, ungodley expensive ball valve that they supplied on the old one. This valve will fail or crack within a few months so don't even bother using it. Get a gate valve instead and throw this pos that Hayward wants $35 for a replacement, right in the trash.
2nd filter came with 2 hoses. While I didn't expect this, one of them leaked right from the start. My option was to send the whole filter back to Amazon and order another, or buy a replacement hose locally. I chose the latter. Hayward only supplies ONE valve, the crappy ball valve described above. Apparently they think you should choose between whether you want your pool area flooded when you clean out the hair and lint trap, OR have your pool area flooded when you drain the filter. With one valve, you cannot protect both operations. Of course with the crappy valve they do furnish, you'll be flooding any way you look at it.
While the principles behind the filter work well, Hayward as a company has seemingly chosen to create a profit center off of replacement parts. For as many times as you use a shutoff valve, they should last decades, NOT MONTHS.
Furnishing crappy hoses that leak right out of the box spells out the character of the company, and it ain't good. Perhaps the founders of the company had good intentions when they designed the original filter, but the people running the company now seem to prefer to use crappy attachments to an otherwise quality product.
top_profane.title: I am so done with Hayward

top_profane.text:
I'm disappointed. I paid $341 for what's described as a "tilt out trash bin", and the picture of the product shows a tilt out trash bin holder WITH a trash bin. When I took delivery and opened the box, there's no trash bin! All I got was the stainless steel trash bin HOLDER. Now I've got to drive all over town looking for a 12X14X19" trash bin that matches the holder shape, a $10 item (EXTRA WORK...potentially hours) just to turn this product into an actual working "trash bin", WITH A BIN! How lame is that? On the good side, the quality of the stainless steel and welding seems good, but I just got it, haven't mounted it. And speaking of mounting, the mounting tabs are too shallow. The mounting flanges should to be designed so the flanges go 2" deeper for more versatile mounting options for BBQ island frames. These flanges are so shallow that my stacked stone on the BBQ island make the trash bin a bugger to mount (extra work also). I guess I'll have to make custom extended mounting frame out of pine to push out the bin so my stacked stone mates flush to the holder edges like my other doors and drawers (which have deeper flange mounts). This trash bin appears to be designed to mount only to a wood frame, and I'm trying to avoid using wood in my Island. One thing I like about this trash bin holder is the handle "somewhat" matches my Cal Flame doors/drawer handles. Cal Flame doesn't make a tilt out trash bin, the OCI handle matching Cal Flame products was a key attraction for me, actually it was the deal clincher. I didn't buy OCI doors because Cal Flame (Costco Online)doors are considerably cheaper and are double walled, which make them rigid and strong. The OCI "appear" to be double walled doors in their website videos, however they don't state this in their feature list (bad marketing), so I was unsure. I got my Cal Flame doors from Costco, they are very nice, and the handles are awesome, very expensive feeling. However, I read that Cal Flame service isn't good. I hope I don't have a problem.

UPDATE
After hours of searching I found the right trash can bin for the OCI bin holder. I sent OCI the product information in case they want to include the plastic trash bin like their ad picture implies. The tilt out bin holder from OCI works great, looks great. As for the mounting flanges, I had to make a wooden frame due to the design of the frame of the bin holder. This product was not designed for BBQ islands with stacked stone. Although the quality of this product is good material-wise, the design could have been better for virtually the same price to manufacture. Lastly, Costco doesn't carry Cal Flame anymore, they have another brand now called Urban Island, which I believe to be made by Bull BBQ in Ontario,CA.

top_profane.text:
I'm going to model my review off another and categorize my comments. I'm throwing in a little humor to keep you interested! I'm not making a mockery of the grill because this grill, in the words of John Lennon, is "just a buttered scone and nice cup of treacle" (that makes no sense, but mentioning John Lennon makes it seem more "hip").

I purchased this grill in the summer of 2011 and have been using it for about the last 5-6 months, so this is not an impulsive review written simply because it arrived and it looks pretty. I've used it heavily and, by god, I know what in the sam-hill I'm talking about!

DELIVERY:
With Amazon Prime shipping, I received the grill in about 72 hours. It was heavy as a busload of overfed nuns on their way to a Weight Watchers meeting, but it was shipped by a local moving company and delivery was no hassle at all. They called to schedule a time for delivery and dumped the 200 pound box in my garage with no problems. The delivery guy (who smelled like he'd been working in the tobacco fields of a West Virginia plantation) asked me to inspect it before leaving to make sure it was in good condition. It was. With a hearty handshake, I sent him on his stinky way. No, seriously, this dude's B.O. was rank! Like, latrine rank after spicy burrito night at the mess hall...

ASSEMBLY:
Assembly is extremely easy. I put it together myself in about an hour, working slowly while watching the game on TV (OK, you got me, I was watching ballet... OK OK OK, it was porn). As others have mentioned, it's a good idea to have your own socket set and wrench set handy. Your own tools will work far better than the ones provided. If you have a cordless drill or cordless impact driver with a socket insert, it'll work great and save you 45 minutes of hand-twisting (unless you're watching what I was watching and you'll still need to do some hand twisting later).

NATURAL GAS VS. PROPANE:
If you're one of those people who swears by propane because you think it burns hotter, burns cleaner, is easier to use, burns up the bones of your unsuspecting victims, or whatever, this grill changes all of that. Hooked up to natural gas, I've had this grill up to 700 degrees. It will get hotter than you'll ever need. Also, with a natural gas hookup, you'll never run out of fuel in the middle of preparing a nice juicy yak steak. You'll never have to make a trip to buy a new canister of propane because you're not sure how much is left. Also, depending on how much you grill, the cost will probably be less than that of propane. I've left this grill on overnight (to burn off BBQ sauce or whatever and just forgot that it was on) several times in a month and never noticed much of an increase in my gas bill. This thing must be unbelievably efficient. Also, if you worry about location and want to move the grill away from your deck or whatever to grill, it comes with a 12 foot (maybe 20 foot, I forgot) natural gas hose, so you've got a good distance to wheel this beast around if need be.

PREHEATING:
Getting the grill to 400 takes about 5 minutes. Up to 700 took about 10-12 minutes. If you need your grill to heat up faster than that, you either need to cook on a jet engine or go on a diet because your fattiness is screwing up your level of patience. Yeah yeah, I'm a fatso too, so quit your whining about being politically correct, you Nancy boy.

COOKING:
It's true that this grill makes cooking too easy. I know others have said it and I thought it was just a huge load of crap until I got the grill. The flavorizer bars disperse the heat so perfectly that it cooks evenly everywhere on the surface.

OPERATION AND FEATURES (Ignition, SearStation, Side Burner, and Rotisserie):
1> The push start ignition is the best I've found so far. I've had several grills and that's always the first thing to crap out. I've had ones that were stock and came with the grill as well as add-on ignition systems I've installed as an aftermarket part. They all dumped out within 6 months only to leave me standing there with a match cursing the designers, their families, and their future descendants. This one works perfectly still. Granted, I've only been using the grill for six months, so it might crap out any second now, but it still fires up on the first push every time. I can't say that about any of the other grills I've had (aside from my Coleman camping grills, stoves, and griddles with the InstaStart).
2> The SearStation function is pretty nifty. It's basically a fourth burner that gets this grill hotter than the blazes of hell and damnation itself! Crank that bad boy up, drop your meat, sear the stank off it, flip it, and sear the other side. After flipping it, just turn off that burner and the grill will drop down to perfect cooking temperature in just the right amount of time it takes to finish that sear. The odd part is that the SearStation knob is much smaller than the regular burner knobs. In my mind, the SearStation knob should be five times bigger than everything else and read "VOLCANO SETTING... No, really... It's friggin' hot, man!"
3> The side burner is pretty standard stuff. One knob, push start, and viola!, burny burny. Not bad, but pretty pointless if there's any kind of wind beyond the standard exit-velocity of a fart. I've used it twice and wasn't too impressed. It probably works well with a small saucepan or frying pan, but it took forever to heat up 4 cups of water to make my pasta side.
4> Rotisserie. Holy crap! Get it. GET IT NOW! Buy the rotisserie and partake of flavors that only gods were meant to enjoy. Seriously. One thing I like on the rotisserie is a pork roast, lightly coated in a smoky mustard, and then wrapped in bacon. Yeah, it's not for heart patients, but who doesn't love bacon? (If you don't love bacon, stop reading this review right now! You don't deserve to read any more of my literary masterpiece.) Honestly though, it takes a few tries to get the temperature right and everything, but once you figure out the best way to rotisserie (is that even a verb?), you'll wonder how you ever got along without it.

DURABILITY:
As you know, you never want to throw your grill cover onto a hot grill. I've forgotten to put the cover on a few times. That grill has had rain, hail, and freezing rain dumped on it. Granted, it's only 6 months old, but it looks brand new. Just wipe it down with a cloth and you're good to go! The finish really seems to hold up to the weather. Also, the interior parts look brand new as well. Leave it on high for a couple hours and everything burns right off. I swear on a stack of bibles that the inside of my grill looks like it's only been used 5 or 6 times. It's got a little "grilling patina" on it, but definitely doesn't show the wear of about 50 different meals I've prepared with it.

WEBER SUPPORT:
These people are very nice. Not sappy Mary Poppins "stick up the rear" nice, but nice. When my grill arrived, I was missing the gas coupling. I was madder than a red-breasted hen at a bluebeard convention (I don't even know what the heck that means, but it sounds like something a standard hillbilly would say). So, I called up just before 3 PM, the lady listened to my problem, and I had the part in my hot little hairy-palmed hands by noon the next day (keep in mind though that I only live about 40 minutes from the Weber plant - but it was nice that she got it out the same day even though she claimed the last pick-up was at 3:00 - so she must have really leaned on someone to get that part out since she told me "I'll try to get it out, but our last pick-up is in about 3 minutes"). So, Weber customer service is good! VERY GOOD!

HELPFUL HINTS:
1> I don't know if they still make them, but Weber used to have a whole series of very thin cookbooks. They were like 9x12, but only a quarter inch thick. I got these as a gift a few years ago and would strongly recommend picking them up. They're overpriced (which is why I asked for them as a gift), but they've got great recipes for your grill. The recipes in there are spectacular AND, it may sound strange, but the recipes taste better on this grill. I made a few of the dishes from these books on my old grill and they were good, but on this Weber grill, they just seem to come to life... dead, tasty, juicy death, but metaphorical life, if you will.
2> Get a cover! That's a no-brainer. You're spending $800 on a grill. Don't be a penny-pinching miser and leave your cheese out blowing in the wind because you're too cheap to buy a $20-$30 cover.
3> Get the Weber handle light. It looks small and it looks cheap, but it really lights up your grilling surface. I highly recommend it!! In fact, as a lover and huge proponent of yogurt enemas, I recommend the handle light even more highly than a good colon busting yogurt blower.
4> Get a good grill brush. Don't just buy the cheapest piece of wire-infused hickory you can find. Get one with long bristles that go right to the end of the brush (so you can get the edges really good). You could probably also get a softer wire brush from a hardware store that might do the job better. But overall, crank up the grill to maximum heat for an hour and then hit it with the brush and it'll look like it's brand new.
5> After putting the grill together, I know you're going to want to fire it up right away. Don't. Season your grates. Rub some olive oil on them and let them sit for awhile (maybe even do that before you start assembly). Then, once they've had awhile to sit with the olive oil, crank that bad boy up and you're good to go. Re-season the cooking grates while they're cold (with olive oil on a basting brush) after every 10 uses or so and it'll making grilling that much easier.
6> Don't be an idiot and say, "Eh, I don't need to fill out that warranty nonsense." Do it! They're easy to deal with if something goes wrong and they stand behind their product!

Enjoy your new grill and if you buy it because of my review, hoist a cold one as you bite into the first steak you cook on it and say, "Gary, you were right! Thank you, you fat, white, hairy grill-mastering son of a turkey testicle!"

And remember to join my campaign for a new Weber slogan: "Weber, with a grill this good, any schmuck can cook a frickin' steak!"
top_profane.title: One Impressive Meat Holocaust Machine!

top_profane.text:
This appears to have done the trick. You have to know a few things about ghost ants. They're greedy, horny, snobby little bastards. The ones you see- they're all female, the males only live a week, and only live to impregnate the females. The females live a very long time, they live to breed, and when different colonies meet- unlike other ants that wipe each other out- ghost ants hook up, share males, and get to partying *together.* Which means an infestation gets out of hand really fast, as it has in my house. All exterior walls, every hole 1mm in diameter, these greedy little bastards moved in. I tried every granule poison Home Depot supplies, nothing. I tried baking soda with sugar, it killed a few, but the majority weren't interested, they certainly didn't carry it back to their friends. Petulant, greedy, bastards. A professional service came out and put down Terro Boric Acid, and that seemed to work, but my infestation was too large (read: expensive) for the amount of work required.

So I bought this stuff, and spent 2 hours working around my house, putting tiny little drops (they seem to like tiny drops better than big pools) everywhere along the hundreds of pheromone trails, and squeezing a little droplet into each opening in the brickwork that they were pouring out of. As you can tell from my phone pictures, they were drawn to it like a Hollywood actor to cocaine. Like, immediately. These greedy little bastards sucked it up, walked off, now they're piling up dead at the base of the walls. It appears they lived long enough to tell their other greedy little bastard friends, because the piles are growing. I have a 4000 square foot house, I still only went through about half the bottle on the whole exterior. Little drops everywhere. So while the $18/bottle seemed steep at first, I can attest it is proper value. I have very large infestations in all my Mexican Fan palms as well, I have put a few drops strategically throughout the fronds and base, I'll report back if it kills the fronds/trees. That would suck, but this is war.

This stuff won't survive rain or cleaning, and evidently ghost ants are connoiseurs of boric acid as well, so frequent reapplication of fresh bait is recommended by the greater internet. I'm just hoping this stuff is carried back and the nests die. I'll report back on the progress. Happy hunting!!!

ANT PSYCHOLOGY 101: Apartments

Q: How do you remove ants in a four unit building?
A: Study your enemy, learn how it thinks.

If you have to sum up ants in one word, greed. It's their greatest strength, and their greatest weakness.

Day One: Put two Terro baits in the apartment. One in the kitchen, one in the bathroom, as this was where they were active. Bastards were actually attracted to my sink drains. Keeping a clean home means nothing when the rotten bastards are interested in consuming tap water and fermenting hair in the sink drain.

Day Five: I have conquered most of their forces, yet the enemy continues its tiny assaults in small platoons. I was sitting around wondering why my Terro experience wasn't as awesome as all the other reviews. It's because the bastard ants had other supply trains in the other apartment units. Well then... time to exploit their weakness...

Day Six: I set out four new terro baits in each DMZ. Some bastard ant platoon must have blitzed in their ant pants when they discovered this cornucopia of delicious Borax booze. They chose to party in the kitchen, sending all of their units and apparently having a typical Roman Saturnalia next to my sink.

Day Seven: Bastards are dead. Now I can leave my dishes out after dinner and be lazy about washing them until the morning! USA USA USA!
top_profane.title: It's their greatest strength, and their greatest weakness

top_profane.text:
These devices will make using garden hoses much less of a hassle. Connecting hoses to sill-cocks, to other hoses, to nozzles, or to sprinklers will be quicker and more secure. There are plastic versions available, but I'd suggest the brass. You know what you have to connect, so you can figure the number of fittings in both pairs and singly. If your sill cocks are close to the ground, you may want to increase convenience by getting swivel gooseneck fittings -- also Amazon items -- but if you live in a frost-prone area, make sure the goosenecks are open. Even if you have frost-proof sill-cocks, there can still be freezing issues unless you use the open goosenecks.
I'd recommend keeping the fittings mud/dirt free. I'd also recommend shutting off the sill-cock and opening the nozzle before removing the fitting from the sill-cock.
Yes, there is one more recommendation: Always leave the hoses empty. Not only are they easier to roll up, but there's no chance of the breaks that can happen in charged hoses in the sun.
top_profane.title: Hose-Hassle Reducers.
